CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A : Questions number 1 to 5 are very short answer questions and carry 1 mark each.
  3. Section B : Questions number 6 to 12 are short answer questions and carry 2 marks each.
  4. Section C : Questions number 13 to 24 are also short answer questions and carry 3 marks each.
  5. Section D : Questions number 25 to 27 are long answer questions and carry 5 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions of one mark, two questions of two marks, four questions of three marks and all the three questions of five marks weightage. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
  7. Use of log tables, if necessary. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?

Solution:

Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble).
Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C1 of one glucose unit and C4 of the next glucose unit.

 1

OR

Write the products obtained after hydrolysis of DNA.

Solution:

Hydrolysis of DNA gives 2-deoxyribose, nitrogen containing heterocyclic base( Adenine, Guanine, Cytosine and Thymine), phosphoric acid.

 1
2.

Arrange the following in increasing order of pKb, values:
C6H5CH2NH2, C6H5NHCH3, C6H5NH2

Solution:

C6H5CH2NH2 < C6H5NHCH3 < C6H5NH2

 1
3.

What type of colloid is formed when a liquid is dispersed in a solid? Give an example.

Solution:

The colloid which is formed when a liquid is dispersed in a solid is gel, e.g butter.

 1
4.

Out of Chlorobenzene and p-nitrochlorobenzene, which one is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction and why?

Solution:

p-Nitrochlorobenzene  is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction due to electron withdrawing nature of -NO2 group.

 1
5.

Out of KCl and AgCl, which one shows Schottky defect and why?

Solution:

Chapter deleted from the textbook.

 1

OR

Why does ZnO appear yellow on heating?

Solution:

ZnO is white at room temperature. ZnO loses oxygen on heating. The excess of Zn2+ ions moves to interstitial sites and vacant sites of anions are occupied by electrons which absorb light from visible region and radiate complementary colour i.e yellow.

{tex}ZnO\xrightarrow\triangle\;Zn^{2+}\;+\frac12O_2\;+2e^-{/tex}

 1

SECTION B
6.

When FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A). Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B). Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C). An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D). Identify (A), (B), (C) and (D).

Solution:

FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A).

Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B).

Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C).

An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D).


Therefore,
A = Na2CrO4 = Sodium chromate,
B= Na2Cr2O7 = Sodium dichromate,
C= K2Cr2O7 = Potassium dichromate,
D= Na2SO4 = Sodium sulfate

 2
7.

Give reasons:

  1. A decrease in temperature is observed on mixing ethanol and acetone.
  2. Potassium chloride solution freezes at a lower temperature than water.
Solution:
  1. When ethanol is mixed with acetone, it shows positive deviation from Raoult's law and acetone molecules get in between the host molecules and break some hydrogen bonds, which requires higher energy than energy released in the formation of new hydrogen bonds. This results fall in temperature.
  2. According to Raoult's law, when a non-volatile solid is added to the solvent, its vapour pressure decreases resulting in decrease in freezing point. Also, the freezing point of water is higher than when water contains KCl.
 2
8.

Write balanced chemical equations for the following processes:

  1. Cl2 is passed through slaked lime.
  2. SO2 gas is passed through an aqueous solution of Fe(III) salt.
Solution:
  1. {tex}2Ca(OH)_2 + 2Cl_2 \rightarrow CaCl_2 + Ca(OCl)_2 + 2H_2O{/tex}
  2. {tex}SO_2 + 2Fe^{3+} + 2H_2O  \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+{/tex}
 2

OR
  1. Write two poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Why does Cu2+ solution give blue colour on reaction with ammonia?
Solution:
  1. Mustard gas, tear gas, phosgene are poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Because it forms blue coloured complex [Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
    Reaction: Cu2+ + 4NH3 (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}[Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
 2
9.

Write structures of main compounds A and B in each of the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{PCI}}_5}\mathrm A\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_2/\mathrm{Pd}-{\mathrm{BaSO}}_4}\mathrm B{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}}\mathrm A\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})/\mathrm{conc}.\;\mathrm{HCI}}\mathrm B{/tex}
Solution:
  1. {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{PCI}}_5}\underset{\underset{\mathrm{Benzoyl}\;\mathrm{chloride}}{\lbrack\mathrm A\rbrack}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5\mathrm{COCI}}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_2/\mathrm{Pd}-{\mathrm{BaSO}}_4}\mathrm C\underset{\underset{\mathrm{Benzaldehyde}}{\lbrack\mathrm B\rbrack}}{{}_6{\mathrm H}_5\mathrm{CHO}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm{Acetone}}{\lbrack\mathrm A\rbrack}}{\overset{\overset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})/\mathrm{conc}.\mathrm{HCI}}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\underset{\underset{\mathrm{Propane}}{\lbrack\mathrm B\rbrack}}{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}{\mathrm{CH}}_3+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex}
 2
10.

Define the following terms with a suitable example of each:

  1. Chelate complex
  2. Ambidentate ligand
Solution:
  1. Chelate complex is the complex formed when a di - or polydentate ligand uses its two or more donor atoms to bind a single metal atom.
  2. Ligand which can ligate through two different atoms is called ambidentate ligand.
 2

OR

Using IUPAC norms, write the formulae for the following complexes:

  1. Tetraamminediaquacobalt(III) chloride
  2. Dibromidobis(ethane-1,2-diamine)platinum(IV) nitrate
Solution:
  1. [Co(NH3)4(H2O)2]Cl3
  2. [Pt Br2(en)2](NO3)2
 2
11.
  1. Using valence bond theory, write the hybridization and magnetic character of the complex [Fe(CN)6]4-. (Atomic no. of Fe = 26)
  2. Write the electronic configuration of d6 on the basis of crystal field theory when:
    1. {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} < P and
    2. {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} > P
Solution:
  1. d2sp3, diamagnetic
    1. {tex}t_{2g} {^4e_g^2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}t_{2g} {^6e_g^0}{/tex}
 2

Define order of reaction. Predict the order of reaction in the given graphs:
